content:
@flint v0 hybrid
G: redesign_monolith_into_bounded_services_preserving_order_payment_atomicity
C: no_distributed_transactions ∧ migration_budget("6_months") ∧ team("8_engineers") ∧ stack("postgres_kafka_redis_k8s") ∧ current_domains("orders_billing_fulfillment_inventory") ∧ strong_consistency_required_on_order_to_payment ∧ release_coupling_and_12min_test_suite_today ∧ runway("2_years")
P: colocate_orders_and_billing_in_one_service_with_single_postgres_txn ∧ extract_fulfillment_as_kafka_consumer_of_order_paid_events ∧ extract_inventory_as_sync_grpc_reservation_api_idempotent ∧ ownership_rule("each_service_owns_its_schema_no_shared_tables_no_cross_fks") ∧ sync_grpc_for_consistency_critical_reads_and_inventory_reservation ∧ async_kafka_for_domain_events("order_paid_order_cancelled_shipment_dispatched_stock_reserved") ∧ saga_with_compensations_only_downstream_of_order_billing_never_inside_it ∧ redis_for_idempotency_keys_sessions_and_rate_limits
V: verify("order_and_payment_commit_in_single_postgres_transaction") ∧ verify("no_2pc_or_xa_anywhere_in_stack") ∧ verify("per_service_test_suite_under_3_minutes") ∧ verify("services_deploy_independently_without_coordinated_migrations") ∧ verify("kafka_consumers_idempotent_under_replay") ∧ verify("no_cross_service_foreign_keys_or_shared_tables") ∧ verify("inventory_reservation_api_idempotent_via_client_token") ∧ verify("rollback_path_exists_per_migration_phase")
A: month_1("extract_inventory_via_strangler_fig_dual_read") ∧ month_2("extract_fulfillment_consuming_kafka_from_monolith") ∧ months_3_4("carve_order_billing_boundary_migrate_schema_in_place") ∧ month_5("cutover_dual_write_then_flip_reads") ∧ month_6("decommission_monolith_modules_stabilize") ∧ adopt_event_schema_registry_for_kafka_contracts ∧ adopt_transactional_outbox_in_order_billing_for_reliable_event_emission ∧ k8s_namespace_per_service_with_independent_ci_pipeline

[AUDIT]
Three services, not four. Orders and billing stay fused behind a single Postgres transaction — that is how strong consistency survives the "no distributed transactions" constraint. Fulfillment and inventory split out as independent services: inventory exposes a synchronous idempotent gRPC reservation API (checkout path needs an answer now), fulfillment consumes kafka events (order_paid, order_cancelled) and runs sagas with compensations. Ownership rule is strict: each service owns its schema, no shared tables, no cross-service foreign keys, cross-service reads go through APIs or kafka-fed read projections. Outbox pattern inside order_billing ensures event emission is atomic with the payment commit. Migration is strangler-fig over six months: extract inventory first (lowest coupling, read-heavy), then fulfillment (already event-friendly), then carve the order_billing boundary in place during months 3-4, dual-write and flip in month 5, decommission in month 6. Redis handles idempotency keys, sessions, rate limits. Each service gets its own k8s namespace and CI pipeline, which is what breaks release coupling and the 12-minute test suite.

content:
@flint v0 hybrid
G: define_exclusive_table_ownership_per_service_with_documented_read_paths_and_events
C: one_owner_per_table ∧ services("order_billing_fulfillment_inventory_customers") ∧ no_cross_service_foreign_keys ∧ no_distributed_transactions ∧ tables("orders_order_items_payments_shipments_inventory_holds_customers") ∧ sync_grpc_and_async_kafka_only ∧ strong_consistency_preserved_for_order_to_payment ∧ events_carry_snapshots_to_avoid_back_queries
P: order_billing_owns_exclusive("orders_order_items_payments") ∧ fulfillment_owns_exclusive("shipments") ∧ inventory_owns_exclusive("inventory_holds_and_stock_levels") ∧ customers_owns_exclusive("customers_addresses_payment_method_tokens") ∧ order_billing_reads("customer_profile_via_sync_grpc_at_checkout_and_inventory_reservation_via_sync_grpc") ∧ fulfillment_reads("order_line_items_and_shipping_address_from_order_paid_event_payload_only_no_back_queries") ∧ inventory_reads_no_external_authoritative_data_only_serves_reservation_rpc ∧ customers_reads_no_external_data_is_pure_source_of_truth
V: verify("each_of_six_tables_has_exactly_one_owner_service") ∧ verify("no_cross_service_foreign_keys_in_any_schema") ∧ verify("fulfillment_never_queries_order_billing_db_or_api_for_line_items") ∧ verify("customer_profile_read_path_serves_stale_projection_when_customers_service_down") ∧ verify("inventory_reservation_idempotent_under_retry_via_client_token") ∧ verify("order_paid_event_contract_includes_line_items_amounts_and_shipping_snapshot") ∧ verify("events_emitted_via_transactional_outbox_atomic_with_state_change") ∧ verify("schema_registry_blocks_backward_incompatible_event_changes")
A: order_billing_publishes("order_placed_order_paid_order_cancelled_payment_failed_order_refunded") ∧ fulfillment_publishes("shipment_created_shipment_dispatched_shipment_delivered_shipment_failed") ∧ inventory_publishes("stock_reserved_stock_released_stock_adjusted_low_stock_alert") ∧ customers_publishes("customer_created_customer_updated_customer_deleted_address_changed") ∧ event_payload_rule("order_paid_carries_order_id_line_items_amounts_shipping_address_customer_id") ∧ sync_grpc_endpoints("inventory_reserve_inventory_release_customers_get_profile_customers_get_shipping_address") ∧ denormalized_projection("order_billing_maintains_readonly_customer_view_fed_by_customer_updated_kafka_stream") ∧ enforce("no_service_touches_another_services_schema_via_direct_db_access_or_shared_connection")

[AUDIT]
Four services, six tables, one owner per table. order_billing owns orders, order_items, payments — these three share a single Postgres transaction to preserve atomic order-to-payment. fulfillment owns shipments only. inventory owns inventory_holds (and implicit stock_levels). customers owns the customers table plus addresses and tokenized payment methods. Cross-service reads: order_billing calls customers over sync gRPC at checkout (with a kafka-fed read-only customer projection as fallback for degraded-mode reads) and calls inventory.reserve over sync gRPC on the critical path. fulfillment does not call back — it reads everything it needs from the order_paid event payload, which carries line items, amounts, and a shipping address snapshot. inventory is a pure RPC surface, reads nothing external. customers is a source of truth, reads nothing external. Publishes: order_billing emits the order lifecycle (placed/paid/cancelled/payment_failed/refunded) via transactional outbox so emission is atomic with the DB commit. fulfillment emits shipment lifecycle events. inventory emits stock_reserved/released/adjusted. customers emits profile change events. Hard rule: no cross-service FKs, no direct DB access across services, no back-queries from fulfillment — if it is not in the event payload, it is not available. Schema registry gates kafka contract changes.

Hard rule: no cross-service FKs, no direct DB access across services, no back-queries from fulfillment — if it is not in the event payload, it is not available. Schema registry gates kafka contract changes.

content:
@flint v0 hybrid
G: design_order_to_payment_saga_with_idempotent_steps_and_compensations_no_2pc_no_distributed_locks
C: no_2pc_and_no_distributed_locks ∧ every_step_idempotent_via_client_token ∧ strong_consistency_kept_inside_order_billing_single_postgres_txn ∧ cross_service_coordination_via_saga_with_compensations ∧ inventory_holds_have_ttl ∧ psp_provides_idempotency_keys_and_webhooks ∧ kafka_at_least_once_delivery_with_consumer_dedup ∧ outbox_emits_events_atomic_with_state_change
P: states("cart_submitted_inventory_reserved_payment_authorized_order_confirmed_fulfillment_scheduled_shipped_delivered_cancelled_refunded_failed") ∧ step_1_reserve_inventory("sync_grpc_inventory_reserve_token_order_id_ttl_15min_on_fail_transition_cancelled_out_of_stock") ∧ step_2_authorize_payment("sync_psp_authorize_idempotency_key_order_id_on_fail_compensate_release_inventory_and_cancel_payment_declined") ∧ step_3_capture_and_confirm("single_postgres_txn_in_order_billing_updates_orders_payments_and_writes_outbox_order_paid_event") ∧ step_4_schedule_fulfillment("fulfillment_consumes_order_paid_creates_shipment_idempotent_by_order_id_emits_shipment_created") ∧ step_5_ship_and_deliver("fulfillment_emits_shipment_dispatched_then_shipment_delivered_terminal_state") ∧ compensation_release_inventory("emit_stock_release_command_idempotent_by_order_id_token") ∧ compensation_refund_and_restock("order_billing_issues_psp_refund_emits_order_refunded_which_triggers_inventory_restock_and_shipment_cancel_if_not_yet_dispatched")
V: verify("every_transition_idempotent_under_retry_keyed_by_order_id_plus_step_name") ∧ verify("no_step_requires_distributed_lock_or_2pc_protocol") ∧ verify("payment_timeout_reconciled_via_psp_webhook_plus_poller_never_double_charges") ∧ verify("inventory_hold_expiry_before_capture_triggers_cancel_and_refund_never_ships") ∧ verify("shipment_failed_before_dispatch_triggers_refund_and_restock_after_dispatch_opens_rma_flow") ∧ verify("order_paid_and_payment_captured_commit_atomically_in_one_postgres_transaction") ∧ verify("replay_of_any_kafka_event_produces_same_terminal_state") ∧ verify("every_non_terminal_state_has_defined_compensation_and_timeout")
A: timeout_rule("inventory_reserve_ttl_15min_extended_on_payment_authorized_released_on_payment_declined_or_saga_timeout") ∧ timeout_rule("psp_authorize_deadline_30s_on_timeout_mark_payment_pending_and_reconcile_via_webhook_plus_5min_poller") ∧ timeout_rule("psp_capture_retries_with_same_idempotency_key_until_definitive_response_never_new_key") ∧ idempotency_rule("psp_key_equals_order_id_plus_attempt_number_persisted_in_payments_table_before_rpc_call") ∧ failure_scenario("payment_timeout_leaves_order_in_payment_pending_reconciler_advances_to_authorized_or_declined_never_both") ∧ failure_scenario("inventory_released_after_payment_triggers_order_refunded_and_cancelled_never_ships") ∧ failure_scenario("partial_fulfillment_rollback_if_pre_dispatch_refund_and_restock_if_post_dispatch_open_return_merchandise_authorization_flow") ∧ orchestrator("order_billing_owns_saga_state_persisted_in_orders_state_column_advanced_by_event_handlers_and_rpc_callbacks_no_central_coordinator")

[AUDIT]
Saga state machine lives in order_billing, persisted in orders.state, advanced by rpc callbacks and kafka event handlers — no central coordinator, no distributed lock. Forward path: cart_submitted → inventory_reserved (sync gRPC inventory.reserve, token=order_id, TTL 15min) → payment_authorized (sync PSP call, idempotency key = order_id + attempt, stored in payments before the call so retries reuse it) → order_confirmed + payment_captured (both written in a single Postgres transaction inside order_billing, with order_paid appended to the outbox in that same txn) → fulfillment_scheduled (fulfillment consumes order_paid, creates a shipment keyed by order_id) → shipped → delivered. Compensation path from each state: inventory_reserved but authorize failed → release_inventory + cancel(payment_declined). payment_authorized but capture failed → void_authorization + release_inventory + cancel. order_confirmed but fulfillment cannot proceed → refund + restock. Timeouts: inventory hold 15min, extended when payment_authorized fires, released on payment_declined or saga timeout. PSP authorize deadline 30s — on timeout the order sits in payment_pending and a reconciler (PSP webhook + 5min poller) resolves it to authorized or declined exactly once, because the idempotency key never changes. Critical scenarios: payment timeout = pending state, reconciler decides; never double-charged because the PSP key is stable. Inventory released after payment (hold expired before capture) = emit order_refunded, mark cancelled, never ship. Partial fulfillment rollback = if shipment_failed fires before dispatch, refund + restock; after dispatch it is no longer a saga rollback but an RMA flow. Idempotency is enforced everywhere: inventory.reserve keyed by order_id, PSP keyed by order_id + attempt, kafka consumers dedup by event_id, outbox dedup by outbox row id. Every non-terminal state has a defined compensation and a timeout.

input.audit: Saga state machine lives in order_billing, persisted in orders.state, advanced by rpc callbacks and kafka event handlers — no central coordinator, no distributed lock. Forward path: cart_submitted → inventory_reserved (sync gRPC inventory.reserve, token=order_id, TTL 15min) → payment_authorized (sync PSP call, idempotency key = order_id + attempt, stored in payments before the call so retries reuse it) → order_confirmed + payment_captured (both written in a single Postgres transaction inside order_billing, with order_paid appended to the outbox in that same txn) → fulfillment_scheduled (fulfillment consumes order_paid, creates a shipment keyed by order_id) → shipped → delivered. Compensation path from each state: inventory_reserved but authorize failed → release_inventory + cancel(payment_declined). payment_authorized but capture failed → void_authorization + release_inventory + cancel. order_confirmed but fulfillment cannot proceed → refund + restock. Timeouts: inventory hold 15min, extended when payment_authorized fires, released on payment_declined or saga timeout. PSP authorize deadline 30s — on timeout the order sits in payment_pending and a reconciler (PSP webhook + 5min poller) resolves it to authorized or declined exactly once, because the idempotency key never changes. Critical scenarios: payment timeout = pending state, reconciler decides; never double-charged because the PSP key is stable. Inventory released after payment (hold expired before capture) = emit order_refunded, mark cancelled, never ship. Partial fulfillment rollback = if shipment_failed fires before dispatch, refund + restock; after dispatch it is no longer a saga rollback but an RMA flow. Idempotency is enforced everywhere: inventory.reserve keyed by order_id, PSP keyed by order_id + attempt, kafka consumers dedup by event_id, outbox dedup by outbox row id. Every non-terminal state has a defined compensation and a timeout.

content:
@flint v0 hybrid
G: audit_eventual_consistency_windows_with_bounds_and_mitigations_for_user_visible_flows
C: services_coordinate_via_saga_and_kafka_not_distributed_txn ∧ at_least_once_event_delivery_with_nonzero_consumer_lag ∧ projections_trail_source_of_truth ∧ user_expects_read_your_writes_on_checkout_and_order_history ∧ ui_tolerates_some_staleness_on_browse_flows ∧ strong_consistency_preserved_only_inside_order_billing_postgres_txn ∧ payment_timeout_window_up_to_5min_worst_case ∧ psp_refund_settlement_takes_business_days_out_of_band
P: window_order_history_visibility("bound_500ms_mitigation_serve_own_orders_from_primary_or_use_session_lsn_token_from_write_response_for_60s_after_confirmation") ∧ window_product_page_stock("bound_2s_staleness_mitigation_show_limited_stock_affordance_authoritative_check_only_at_checkout_via_sync_grpc_reserve") ∧ window_order_status_in_customer_ui("bound_2s_mitigation_sse_push_from_order_billing_projection_with_optimistic_client_render_and_reconciliation") ∧ window_customer_profile_staleness_at_checkout("bound_zero_mitigation_sync_grpc_to_customers_on_checkout_path_projection_forbidden_for_billing_and_shipping_address") ∧ window_payment_pending_awaiting_psp("bound_30s_typical_5min_worst_mitigation_ui_shows_processing_disable_retry_idempotent_resubmit_email_on_resolve") ∧ window_hold_expired_before_capture("bound_detection_within_5min_mitigation_extend_hold_on_payment_authorized_event_auto_refund_plus_apology_email_if_race_lost") ∧ window_refund_visibility_vs_bank_settlement("bound_2s_for_order_status_flip_but_3_to_10_business_days_for_bank_mitigation_ui_copy_sets_settlement_expectation") ∧ window_fulfillment_scheduling_lag("bound_5s_mitigation_order_billing_prewrites_fulfillment_pending_row_on_order_paid_flipped_by_shipment_created_event")
V: verify("read_your_writes_for_own_order_history_under_500ms_p99_after_confirmation") ∧ verify("authoritative_stock_check_always_at_checkout_never_browse") ∧ verify("checkout_never_reads_customer_address_from_projection_only_sync_grpc") ∧ verify("payment_pending_ui_prevents_double_submit_while_idempotency_key_stable") ∧ verify("kafka_consumer_lag_under_2s_p99_on_shipment_and_order_status_topics") ∧ verify("refund_ui_copy_distinguishes_order_status_flip_from_bank_settlement") ∧ verify("hold_expired_capture_race_triggers_auto_refund_and_email_within_5min") ∧ verify("every_projection_has_documented_staleness_bound_and_alert_when_exceeded")
A: projection_rule("user_scoped_reads_hit_primary_or_use_lsn_token_returned_in_write_response_for_read_your_writes") ∧ affordance_rule("stock_labels_show_in_stock_limited_stock_out_of_stock_never_exact_count_on_browse") ∧ push_channel("order_billing_exposes_sse_endpoint_client_subscribes_after_confirmation_for_status_updates") ∧ sync_path_rule("profile_address_payment_method_always_sync_grpc_on_checkout_never_projection") ∧ reconciler("payment_pending_poller_every_60s_plus_psp_webhook_resolves_within_5min_slo") ∧ compensation_notification("auto_refund_emits_order_refunded_and_notifications_service_sends_email_with_reason_code") ∧ copy_rule("refund_ui_reads_refund_initiated_expect_3_to_10_business_days_for_bank_settlement") ∧ slo_monitor("prometheus_alert_at_2s_p99_kafka_consumer_lag_and_500ms_p99_order_history_read_your_writes")

[AUDIT]
Eight user-visible eventual-consistency windows that matter, each with a bound and mitigation. (1) Order history after confirmation — user expects to see their order immediately. Bound 500ms p99. Mitigation: own-order reads hit the primary, or the client attaches the LSN token returned in the confirmation response so the read waits for that LSN on the replica. Good for 60s post-write. (2) Product-page stock — projection-fed, drifts ~2s. Never authoritative. Browse shows "in stock / limited / out of stock" affordances, never exact counts; the real check is inventory.reserve at checkout. (3) Order status updates (paid → shipped → delivered) in the customer UI — driven by kafka projections into order_billing. Bound 2s. Push via SSE from the order_billing projection with optimistic client render and reconciliation on mismatch. (4) Customer profile at checkout — zero tolerance for staleness. Address and payment method always read sync gRPC from customers, never from the projection. Projection is analytics-only. (5) Payment pending — unavoidable window while PSP is deciding. Typical 30s, worst 5min. UI shows "processing", disables retry, idempotent resubmit reuses the key, email fires on resolution. (6) Inventory hold expiring between authorize and capture — the nasty case. Hold gets extended on payment_authorized event; if the race is still lost, auto-refund + apology email, detected within 5min by the reconciler. (7) Refund visibility — the order_billing status flips in 2s, but the bank takes 3–10 business days. UI copy must separate these concerns or users complain. (8) Fulfillment scheduling lag — order_billing pre-writes a "fulfillment_pending" row inside its own commit so the UI shows scheduling immediately, then the row flips on the shipment_created event. Every projection must declare a staleness bound, and prometheus alerts when it is exceeded. The general principle: checkout path never uses projections, browse path always does, and user-visible state transitions get SSE pushes rather than polling.

input.audit: Eight user-visible eventual-consistency windows that matter, each with a bound and mitigation. (1) Order history after confirmation — user expects to see their order immediately. Bound 500ms p99. Mitigation: own-order reads hit the primary, or the client attaches the LSN token returned in the confirmation response so the read waits for that LSN on the replica. Good for 60s post-write. (2) Product-page stock — projection-fed, drifts ~2s. Never authoritative. Browse shows "in stock / limited / out of stock" affordances, never exact counts; the real check is inventory.reserve at checkout. (3) Order status updates (paid → shipped → delivered) in the customer UI — driven by kafka projections into order_billing. Bound 2s. Push via SSE from the order_billing projection with optimistic client render and reconciliation on mismatch. (4) Customer profile at checkout — zero tolerance for staleness. Address and payment method always read sync gRPC from customers, never from the projection. Projection is analytics-only. (5) Payment pending — unavoidable window while PSP is deciding. Typical 30s, worst 5min. UI shows "processing", disables retry, idempotent resubmit reuses the key, email fires on resolution. (6) Inventory hold expiring between authorize and capture — the nasty case. Hold gets extended on payment_authorized event; if the race is still lost, auto-refund + apology email, detected within 5min by the reconciler. (7) Refund visibility — the order_billing status flips in 2s, but the bank takes 3–10 business days. UI copy must separate these concerns or users complain. (8) Fulfillment scheduling lag — order_billing pre-writes a "fulfillment_pending" row inside its own commit so the UI shows scheduling immediately, then the row flips on the shipment_created event. Every projection must declare a staleness bound, and prometheus alerts when it is exceeded. The general principle: checkout path never uses projections, browse path always does, and user-visible state transitions get SSE pushes rather than polling.

content:
Over the next six months we are restructuring the orders platform from a single large codebase into four focused services: one that owns the order-and-payment flow end to end, one for fulfillment, one for inventory, and one for customer profiles. The core problem today is that four domains share the same codebase and database, which causes teams to block each other on releases, creates merge conflicts, slows the test suite to twelve minutes, and forces database migrations to coordinate across every deploy. After the split, each team deploys independently, test suites drop to under three minutes per service, and migrations stop holding up unrelated work. Critically, we are keeping orders and payments together inside a single service, because that is the one place in the business where we cannot tolerate partial failure — a customer is either charged and has an order, or neither. Fulfillment and inventory coordinate through events rather than shared database tables, using well-understood patterns that preserve correctness without the fragility of distributed transactions.

The main risks are migration-period risk and eventual-consistency risk. During the six months, we are running the old and new systems in parallel for each domain we extract, with a clear rollback path at every phase; we extract the lowest-risk domain first (inventory), then fulfillment, and tackle the order-and-payment boundary last when the team has the most context. The eventual-consistency risk is that a few user-visible flows — order history appearing after confirmation, inventory counts on browse pages, refund visibility versus actual bank settlement — will have small delays measured in seconds, which we mitigate with push notifications, UI copy, and targeted use of the primary database for the flows where users expect to see their own writes immediately. The timeline is: inventory extracted in month one, fulfillment in month two, order-and-payment boundary carved in months three and four, cutover in month five, decommissioning in month six. Team of eight engineers, with runway that comfortably covers the work and a buffer beyond.

input.audit: Five day-one signals tell the story. (1) Checkout success rate — the business KPI. Target 99%, page if canary runs 0.3 percentage points below control over a 5-minute window; this catches regressions the internal metrics might miss. (2) Order-to-payment atomicity — a correctness invariant, not a latency metric. A minute-cadence SQL scan looking for orders without payments and payments without orders; any nonzero count is a sev-1 page because it is the one thing the architecture was designed to prevent. Same scan also catches duplicate charges, leaked inventory holds, and unmatched refunds. (3) End-to-end saga latency from cart_submitted to order_confirmed, p50 under 2s and p99 under 8s, pages at p99 > 10s sustained 5 minutes. Histogram-bucketed so the dashboard shows distribution shape, not just a single number. (4) Kafka consumer lag per topic, warn at 5s, page at 30s specifically on order_status and shipment topics because those feed user-visible UI. DLQ size and outbox pending count live on the same panel so stuck events surface immediately. (5) Cross-service gRPC error rate per dependency pair — order_billing → inventory, order_billing → customers — warn at 0.3%, page at 0.5% for 2 minutes. Canary comparison is the same query run once per cohort label (canary vs control) and divided; alerts fire on a ratio greater than 1.5. Dashboard is five rows, top-to-bottom: business SLOs, correctness invariants, saga latency breakdown, event stream health, RPC health — so an on-call starting at the top sees "is the business healthy" before drilling into mechanics. Any sev-1 invariant breach auto-drains the canary to zero via feature flag; no human decision required for correctness breaches. Runbook link on every alert.

content:
@flint v0 hybrid
G: audit_jwt_service_rank_by_severity
C: module("auth/jwt_service.py") ∧ lib("pyjwt") ∧ shared_secret("HS256") ∧ algos("HS256,RS256,none") ∧ verify_exp_false ∧ hardcoded_secret
P: crit_alg_none_accepted ∧ crit_alg_confusion_hs_rs ∧ crit_hardcoded_secret_in_source ∧ crit_exp_check_disabled ∧ high_unverified_header_trusted ∧ high_weak_dev_secret ∧ med_missing_iss_aud_nbf_iat ∧ low_role_default_and_no_kid_rotation
V: reject_token("alg=none") ∧ reject_rs256_token_signed_with_public_key_as_hs256 ∧ load_secret_from_env_or_kms ∧ enforce_verify_exp_true ∧ pin_single_algorithm("RS256") ∧ assert_iss_aud_claims ∧ rotate_via_kid_jwks
A: remove("'none' from ALGOS") ∧ decode("algorithms=['RS256']") ∧ set("options={'verify_exp': True, 'require': ['exp','iss','aud','sub']}") ∧ replace("SECRET = os.environ['JWT_PRIVATE_KEY']") ∧ pass("audience= and issuer= to jwt.decode") ∧ drop("get_unverified_header trust path") ∧ add("kid-based key lookup via JWKS")

[AUDIT]
Severity ranking of findings in auth/jwt_service.py:

CRITICAL
1. `'none'` in ALGOS — attacker forges unsigned token; `jwt.decode(..., algorithms=ALGOS)` accepts it. Auth bypass.
2. Algorithm confusion — mixing HS256 and RS256 with the same `SECRET` lets an attacker sign an HS256 token using the RS256 public key as the HMAC secret, forging any identity including admin.
3. Hardcoded secret in source (`'development-secret-do-not-commit'`) — compromises every token if the repo leaks; "TODO rotate" is not a control.
4. `options={'verify_exp': False}` — expired tokens are accepted forever; `exp` in payload is decorative.

HIGH
5. `get_unverified_header` used to pick the algorithm — attacker controls `alg`, so the check `algo not in ALGOS` is trivially satisfied and steers decode toward the weakest allowed algorithm.
6. Weak, low-entropy, human-readable secret — brute-forceable offline even if not leaked.

MEDIUM
7. No `iss`, `aud`, `nbf`, `iat` validation and no `require` list — tokens from other services/environments are accepted; replay window unbounded.

LOW
8. `role` defaults to `'user'` on missing claim (acceptable) but combined with no signature-algorithm pinning, role tampering is trivial via items 1–2. No `kid`/JWKS, so key rotation is impossible without downtime.

Fix: pin `algorithms=['RS256']`, drop `none`, load key from env/KMS, set `verify_exp=True` with `require=['exp','iss','aud','sub']`, pass `audience=` and `issuer=`, remove the unverified-header branch, add `kid`+JWKS for rotation.

@flint v0 hybrid
G: rank_top3_decode_path_attacks_with_payloads
C: fn("verify") ∧ algos_allow("HS256,RS256,none") ∧ key_param("SECRET") ∧ verify_exp_false ∧ header_alg_attacker_controlled ∧ hs_rs_shared_key_slot
P: rank1_alg_none_forgery ∧ rank2_alg_confusion_rs_to_hs ∧ rank3_expired_token_replay_via_verify_exp_false
V: decode_rejects_none ∧ decode_pins_single_asymmetric_algo ∧ decode_enforces_exp_and_require
A: payload1_none("eyJhbGciOiJub25lIiwidHlwIjoiSldUIn0.eyJzdWIiOiJ2aWN0aW0iLCJyb2xlIjoiYWRtaW4iLCJleHAiOjk5OTk5OTk5OTl9.") ∧ payload2_confusion("header={alg:HS256}; body={sub:victim,role:admin,exp:9999999999}; mac=HMAC_SHA256(rsa_public_key_pem, signing_input)") ∧ payload3_replay("any previously-issued admin token with exp in the past; verify() ignores exp so it still authorizes") ∧ fix("algorithms=['RS256'], verify_exp=True, require=['exp','iss','aud','sub'], drop 'none', separate HS/RS key material")

[AUDIT]
Top 3 attacks on verify() ranked by exploitability:

RANK 1 — alg=none forgery (trivial, no secret knowledge)
Header: {"alg":"none","typ":"JWT"} → base64url → `eyJhbGciOiJub25lIiwidHlwIjoiSldUIn0`
Body: {"sub":"victim","role":"admin","exp":9999999999} → `eyJzdWIiOiJ2aWN0aW0iLCJyb2xlIjoiYWRtaW4iLCJleHAiOjk5OTk5OTk5OTl9`
Signature: empty
Full token: `eyJhbGciOiJub25lIiwidHlwIjoiSldUIn0.eyJzdWIiOiJ2aWN0aW0iLCJyb2xlIjoiYWRtaW4iLCJleHAiOjk5OTk5OTk5OTl9.`
Why exploitable: `'none'` is in ALGOS and passed straight into `jwt.decode(..., algorithms=ALGOS)`. No signature required. `require_admin` returns the Token.

RANK 2 — HS/RS algorithm confusion (requires only the RS256 public key, which is by definition public)
1. Fetch the RSA public key PEM (JWKS endpoint, TLS cert chain, or any published location).
2. Craft header `{"alg":"HS256","typ":"JWT"}` and body `{"sub":"victim","role":"admin","exp":9999999999}`.
3. Compute `signing_input = b64url(header) + "." + b64url(body)`.
4. `sig = HMAC_SHA256(key=public_key_pem_bytes, msg=signing_input)` → base64url.
5. Token: `b64url(header).b64url(body).b64url(sig)`.
Why exploitable: the code passes a single `SECRET` as the key and allows HS256 and RS256 simultaneously. PyJWT hands the HS256 path the same `SECRET` bytes; if the deployment ever sets `SECRET` to the public key (common in mixed HS/RS rollouts) the forgery verifies. Rank 2 because it becomes instant-win the moment key material is asymmetric.

RANK 3 — Expired-token replay (requires one captured admin token)
Payload: any real admin JWT previously issued by `sign()`, regardless of `exp`. Example: an admin's token from last year's logs, pastebin, browser history, or a stolen backup.
Why exploitable: `options={'verify_exp': False}` disables the only temporal check. Token remains valid indefinitely; revocation via short TTL is defeated. Lower rank only because it requires capturing a legitimately signed token first.

Fix: `jwt.decode(token, PUBLIC_KEY, algorithms=['RS256'], audience=AUD, issuer=ISS, options={'require': ['exp','iss','aud','sub']})` — and delete the `get_unverified_header` branch entirely.

A: payload3_replay("any previously-issued admin token with exp in the past; verify() ignores exp so it still authorizes")
fix("algorithms=['RS256'], verify_exp=True, require=['exp','iss','aud','sub'], drop 'none', separate HS/RS key material")

Full token: `eyJhbGciOiJub25lIiwidHlwIjoiSldUIn0.eyJzdWIiOiJ2aWN0aW0iLCJyb2xlIjoiYWRtaW4iLCJleHAiOjk5OTk5OTk5OTl9.`

RANK 2 — HS/RS algorithm confusion (requires only the RS256 public key, which is by definition public)
Steps:
1. Fetch the RSA public key PEM (JWKS endpoint, TLS cert chain, or any published location).
2. Craft header `{"alg":"HS256","typ":"JWT"}` and body `{"sub":"victim","role":"admin","exp":9999999999}`.
3. Compute `signing_input = b64url(header) + "." + b64url(body)`.
4. `sig = HMAC_SHA256(key=public_key_pem_bytes, msg=signing_input)` → base64url.
5. Token: `b64url(header).b64url(body).b64url(sig)`.
Why exploitable: the code passes a single `SECRET` as the key and allows HS256 and RS256 simultaneously. PyJWT hands the HS256 path the same `SECRET` bytes; if the deployment ever sets `SECRET` to the public key (common in mixed HS/RS rollouts) the forgery verifies. Even with the current dev string, the allow-both posture is the precondition — rank 2 because it becomes instant-win the moment key material is asymmetric.

RANK 3 — Expired-token replay (requires one captured admin token)

Why exploitable: `options={'verify_exp': False}` disables the only temporal check. Token remains valid indefinitely; revocation via short TTL is defeated. Lower rank only because it requires capturing a legitimately signed token first.

Fix for all three in one change:
`jwt.decode(token, PUBLIC_KEY, algorithms=['RS256'], audience=AUD, issuer=ISS, options={'require': ['exp','iss','aud','sub']})` — and delete the `get_unverified_header` branch entirely.

content:
Pin RS256 for new tokens. Grandfather old HS256 tokens signed before cutover `iat`, reject `none`, enforce `exp`, require `iss`/`aud`. Drop `get_unverified_header` for alg selection — use `iat < GRANDFATHER_UNTIL` only to pick legacy HS verifier.

```python
# auth/jwt_service.py
import os
import time
import jwt
from dataclasses import dataclass

ISSUER = os.environ['JWT_ISSUER']
AUDIENCE = os.environ['JWT_AUDIENCE']

PRIVATE_KEY = os.environ['JWT_PRIVATE_KEY']   # RSA PEM, signs new tokens
PUBLIC_KEY = os.environ['JWT_PUBLIC_KEY']     # RSA PEM, verifies new tokens
LEGACY_HS_SECRET = os.environ['JWT_LEGACY_HS_SECRET']  # old HS256 secret, verify-only

# 30-day grandfather window: tokens with iat before this are verified with the legacy HS256 key.
# After this instant, only RS256 tokens are accepted. Set to now()+30d at deploy time.
GRANDFATHER_UNTIL = int(os.environ['JWT_GRANDFATHER_UNTIL'])  # unix seconds
CUTOVER_IAT = int(os.environ['JWT_CUTOVER_IAT'])              # unix seconds; tokens iat<this => legacy

NEW_ALGO = 'RS256'
LEGACY_ALGO = 'HS256'
REQUIRED_CLAIMS = ['exp', 'iat', 'iss', 'aud', 'sub']


@dataclass
class Token:
    sub: str
    exp: int
    role: str


def sign(sub: str, role: str, ttl: int = 3600) -> str:
    now = int(time.time())
    payload = {
        'sub': sub,
        'role': role,
        'iat': now,
        'exp': now + ttl,
        'iss': ISSUER,
        'aud': AUDIENCE,
    }
    return jwt.encode(payload, PRIVATE_KEY, algorithm=NEW_ALGO)


def _decode_new(token: str) -> dict:
    return jwt.decode(
        token,
        PUBLIC_KEY,
        algorithms=[NEW_ALGO],
        issuer=ISSUER,
        audience=AUDIENCE,
        options={'require': REQUIRED_CLAIMS, 'verify_exp': True, 'verify_signature': True},
    )


def _decode_legacy(token: str) -> dict:
    # Verify-only path for pre-cutover HS256 tokens. Never used to mint new tokens.
    payload = jwt.decode(
        token,
        LEGACY_HS_SECRET,
        algorithms=[LEGACY_ALGO],
        options={'require': ['exp', 'sub'], 'verify_exp': True, 'verify_signature': True},
    )
    iat = payload.get('iat', 0)
    if iat and iat >= CUTOVER_IAT:
        raise jwt.InvalidTokenError('legacy path rejects post-cutover tokens')
    return payload


def verify(token: str) -> Token:
    now = int(time.time())
    try:
        payload = _decode_new(token)
    except jwt.InvalidTokenError as new_err:
        if now >= GRANDFATHER_UNTIL:
            raise
        try:
            payload = _decode_legacy(token)
        except jwt.InvalidTokenError:
            raise new_err
    return Token(
        sub=payload['sub'],
        exp=int(payload['exp']),
        role=payload.get('role', 'user'),
    )


def require_admin(token_str: str) -> Token:
    t = verify(token_str)
    if t.role != 'admin':
        raise PermissionError('not admin')
    return t
```

Key points: `algorithms=[NEW_ALGO]` and `[LEGACY_ALGO]` pinned per path — no `none`, no HS/RS confusion. Legacy path gated by both `GRANDFATHER_UNTIL` (wall clock) and `CUTOVER_IAT` (token-age check) so an attacker can't mint fresh HS256 tokens during the window. After 30 days, legacy branch is dead code and `LEGACY_HS_SECRET` can be rotated/deleted.

Cover 5 vectors: alg=none, HS/RS confusion, expired exp, wrong-key signature, tampered role. Use real RSA keypair via `cryptography`, legacy HS secret for grandfather path, env vars set in fixture. Each test asserts `verify()` raises and `require_admin` rejects spoofed roles.

```python
# tests/test_jwt_service.py
import base64
import hmac
import hashlib
import importlib
import json
import os
import time
import pytest
import jwt
from cryptography.hazmat.primitives.asymmetric import rsa
from cryptography.hazmat.primitives import serialization


def _b64(b: bytes) -> str:
    return base64.urlsafe_b64encode(b).rstrip(b'=').decode()


@pytest.fixture
def keys():
    priv = rsa.generate_private_key(public_exponent=65537, key_size=2048)
    pub = priv.public_key()
    priv_pem = priv.private_bytes(
        encoding=serialization.Encoding.PEM,
        format=serialization.PrivateFormat.PKCS8,
        encryption_algorithm=serialization.NoEncryption(),
    ).decode()
    pub_pem = pub.public_bytes(
        encoding=serialization.Encoding.PEM,
        format=serialization.PublicFormat.SubjectPublicKeyInfo,
    ).decode()
    return priv_pem, pub_pem


@pytest.fixture
def svc(monkeypatch, keys):
    priv_pem, pub_pem = keys
    now = int(time.time())
    monkeypatch.setenv('JWT_ISSUER', 'test-iss')
    monkeypatch.setenv('JWT_AUDIENCE', 'test-aud')
    monkeypatch.setenv('JWT_PRIVATE_KEY', priv_pem)
    monkeypatch.setenv('JWT_PUBLIC_KEY', pub_pem)
    monkeypatch.setenv('JWT_LEGACY_HS_SECRET', 'legacy-hs-secret-for-tests')
    monkeypatch.setenv('JWT_GRANDFATHER_UNTIL', str(now + 30 * 86400))
    monkeypatch.setenv('JWT_CUTOVER_IAT', str(now))
    import auth.jwt_service as m
    importlib.reload(m)
    return m


def _forge_alg_none(sub='victim', role='admin') -> str:
    header = _b64(json.dumps({'alg': 'none', 'typ': 'JWT'}).encode())
    body = _b64(json.dumps({
        'sub': sub, 'role': role,
        'iat': int(time.time()), 'exp': int(time.time()) + 3600,
        'iss': 'test-iss', 'aud': 'test-aud',
    }).encode())
    return f'{header}.{body}.'


def _forge_hs_using_pubkey(pub_pem: str, sub='victim', role='admin') -> str:
    header = _b64(json.dumps({'alg': 'HS256', 'typ': 'JWT'}).encode())
    body = _b64(json.dumps({
        'sub': sub, 'role': role,
        'iat': int(time.time()), 'exp': int(time.time()) + 3600,
        'iss': 'test-iss', 'aud': 'test-aud',
    }).encode())
    signing_input = f'{header}.{body}'.encode()
    sig = hmac.new(pub_pem.encode(), signing_input, hashlib.sha256).digest()
    return f'{header}.{body}.{_b64(sig)}'


def test_happy_path_rs256(svc):
    tok = svc.sign('alice', 'admin', ttl=60)
    t = svc.verify(tok)
    assert t.sub == 'alice' and t.role == 'admin'
    assert svc.require_admin(tok).sub == 'alice'


def test_alg_none_rejected(svc):
    tok = _forge_alg_none(role='admin')
    with pytest.raises(jwt.InvalidTokenError):
        svc.verify(tok)


def test_alg_confusion_hs_with_public_key_rejected(svc, keys):
    _, pub_pem = keys
    tok = _forge_hs_using_pubkey(pub_pem, role='admin')
    with pytest.raises(jwt.InvalidTokenError):
        svc.verify(tok)


def test_mismatched_algo_rs384_rejected(svc, keys):
    priv_pem, _ = keys
    now = int(time.time())
    tok = jwt.encode(
        {'sub': 'alice', 'role': 'user', 'iat': now, 'exp': now + 60,
         'iss': 'test-iss', 'aud': 'test-aud'},
        priv_pem, algorithm='RS384',
    )
    with pytest.raises(jwt.InvalidTokenError):
        svc.verify(tok)


def test_expired_token_rejected(svc, keys):
    priv_pem, _ = keys
    past = int(time.time()) - 3600
    tok = jwt.encode(
        {'sub': 'alice', 'role': 'user', 'iat': past - 60, 'exp': past,
         'iss': 'test-iss', 'aud': 'test-aud'},
        priv_pem, algorithm='RS256',
    )
    with pytest.raises(jwt.ExpiredSignatureError):
        svc.verify(tok)


def test_forged_signature_with_wrong_key_rejected(svc):
    attacker_priv = rsa.generate_private_key(public_exponent=65537, key_size=2048)
    attacker_pem = attacker_priv.private_bytes(
        encoding=serialization.Encoding.PEM,
        format=serialization.PrivateFormat.PKCS8,
        encryption_algorithm=serialization.NoEncryption(),
    ).decode()
    now = int(time.time())
    tok = jwt.encode(
        {'sub': 'victim', 'role': 'admin', 'iat': now, 'exp': now + 60,
         'iss': 'test-iss', 'aud': 'test-aud'},
        attacker_pem, algorithm='RS256',
    )
    with pytest.raises(jwt.InvalidSignatureError):
        svc.verify(tok)


def test_admin_role_spoof_via_payload_tamper_rejected(svc):
    tok = svc.sign('mallory', 'user', ttl=60)
    header_b64, body_b64, sig_b64 = tok.split('.')
    body = json.loads(base64.urlsafe_b64decode(body_b64 + '=='))
    body['role'] = 'admin'
    tampered_body = _b64(json.dumps(body, separators=(',', ':')).encode())
    tampered = f'{header_b64}.{tampered_body}.{sig_b64}'
    with pytest.raises(jwt.InvalidSignatureError):
        svc.verify(tampered)
    with pytest.raises(jwt.InvalidSignatureError):
        svc.require_admin(tampered)


def test_legacy_hs_token_accepted_within_grandfather(svc):
    now = int(time.time())
    legacy = jwt.encode(
        {'sub': 'legacy-user', 'role': 'user',
         'iat': now - 86400, 'exp': now + 3600},
        os.environ['JWT_LEGACY_HS_SECRET'], algorithm='HS256',
    )
    t = svc.verify(legacy)
    assert t.sub == 'legacy-user'


def test_legacy_hs_token_minted_after_cutover_rejected(svc):
    now = int(time.time())
    fresh_legacy = jwt.encode(
        {'sub': 'attacker', 'role': 'admin',
         'iat': now + 10, 'exp': now + 3600},
        os.environ['JWT_LEGACY_HS_SECRET'], algorithm='HS256',
    )
    with pytest.raises(jwt.InvalidTokenError):
        svc.verify(fresh_legacy)
```

Notes: `importlib.reload` re-runs module top-level with patched env. Last test closes the grandfather-window bypass — attacker with leaked legacy secret can't mint fresh admin tokens because `_decode_legacy` rejects `iat >= CUTOVER_IAT`.

**Memorandum: Authentication Vulnerability in Session-Token Service**

During a routine audit of our authentication module, the security team identified several serious flaws in the component that issues and verifies the session tokens used across our products. In plain terms, the code that is supposed to confirm a user is who they claim to be can be tricked into accepting forged credentials. The most severe of these flaws allows an outsider, with no prior access, to hand-craft a token that names any account — including an administrator account — and have our systems accept it as genuine. A second flaw keeps previously issued tokens valid long after they were meant to expire, and a third would let an attacker who has obtained certain public information impersonate a privileged user. No code execution or database breach is required to exploit these issues; a basic understanding of the token format is sufficient.

The business risk is material. An attacker who successfully forges an administrator token gains the same level of access as our own staff: they can read and modify customer data, alter account settings, and take actions attributed to legitimate users and employees. Because the forged activity would appear as valid, authenticated traffic in our logs, detection after the fact would be difficult and attribution almost impossible. The reputational impact of an incident in this category, particularly for enterprise customers whose contracts include security representations, would be significant, and recovery would likely require customer notification, forced credential rotation, and an external incident report.

We recommend treating the fix as a priority-one deployment and shipping it within the next 24 to 48 hours. The remediation is well understood, contained to a single module, and includes a 30-day grandfather window so that tokens already in circulation continue to work for legitimate users while the new, hardened code takes over for all new sessions. Regression tests covering each of the identified attack paths are in place, and the change has no expected impact on end-user experience. Deployment can proceed through our normal release process; an emergency change window is not required.

Regarding disclosure obligations, our current assessment is that none are triggered at this time. The vulnerabilities were found internally through proactive review, and we have no evidence, in logs or elsewhere, that they were exploited against production traffic. Under that assumption, contractual and regulatory notification thresholds — which generally require a confirmed unauthorized access to personal data — are not met. We will, however, complete a log review across the affected time window before closing the matter, and if any evidence of exploitation emerges, the legal and compliance teams will be engaged immediately to evaluate notification duties under GDPR, state breach-notification statutes, and our enterprise customer agreements. A brief written follow-up will be circulated once that review is complete.

@flint v0 hybrid
G: diagnose_checkout_500_outage
C: outage_window_23min ∧ endpoint("/api/checkout") ∧ pool_size("20") ∧ acquire_timeout("5000ms") ∧ reaper_scans("~8M rows") ∧ reaper_cron_interval("~23min") ∧ p99_spike("340ms->5047ms") ∧ error_rate("0.97")
P: correlate_reaper_start_with_pool_exhaustion ∧ observe("22:04:18 reaper start == pool active=20 waiting=47+") ∧ observe("22:04:22 reaper done == pool waiting=0 recovered") ∧ observe("22:27 and 22:50 same pattern, ~23min cadence") ∧ infer_reaper_holds_pool_connection_during_full_scan ∧ infer_long_scan_starves_checkout_acquires ∧ rule_out_traffic_spike("recovery is instant on reaper finish, not load-shed") ∧ root_cause("reaper shares app pool and holds connection for 4.3s full-table scan, draining pool")
V: check_scheduler_cron("expected ~23min interval") ∧ check_reaper_query_plan("full scan on sessions, missing index on expires_at") ∧ check_pool_metrics_during_next_reaper_window ∧ confirm_reaper_uses_same_datasource_as_checkout ∧ repro("trigger reaper manually, watch pool active saturate")
A: isolate_reaper_to_dedicated_datasource_or_readonly_pool ∧ batch_reaper("delete where expires_at<now limit 10000 loop") ∧ add_index("sessions.expires_at") ∧ shorten_reaper_transaction_scope ∧ alert_on_pool_waiting_gt("10")

[AUDIT]
Every ~23 min the session reaper starts a full-table scan over ~8M rows on the same connection pool used by /api/checkout. The reaper holds a pool connection for its entire 4.3s run, which (combined with whatever lock/IO pressure the scan creates) drains the 20-connection pool — checkout threads then block on acquire and hit the 5000ms timeout, producing the 500 wave. The moment the reaper finishes, the pool recovers instantly (waiting=0, p99 back to 380ms), which rules out a traffic spike: the outage tracks the reaper 1:1. Fix is to stop letting the reaper compete with live traffic — give it its own datasource, batch the delete, and add an index on expires_at so the scan is cheap.

P: observe("22:04:18 reaper start == pool active=20 waiting=47+")
observe("22:04:22 reaper done == pool waiting=0 recovered")
rule_out_traffic_spike("recovery is instant on reaper finish, not load-shed")
root_cause("reaper shares app pool and holds connection for 4.3s full-table scan, draining pool")
V: check_reaper_query_plan("full scan on sessions, missing index on expires_at")
repro("trigger reaper manually, watch pool active saturate")

The reaper holds a pool connection for its entire 4.3s run, which (combined with whatever lock/IO pressure the scan creates) drains the 20-connection pool — checkout threads then block on acquire and hit the 5000ms timeout, producing the 500 wave. The moment the reaper finishes, the pool recovers instantly (waiting=0, p99 back to 380ms), which rules out a traffic spike: the outage tracks the reaper 1:1. Fix is to stop letting the reaper compete with live traffic — give it its own datasource, batch the delete, and add an index on expires_at so the scan is cheap.</audit>
</invoke>

content:
@flint v0 hybrid
G: hypothesize_reaper_checkout_pool_interaction
C: shared_datasource("reaper and checkout share same pool") ∧ reaper_single_txn("holds 1 of 20 connections for ~4.3s") ∧ reaper_full_scan("~8M rows, likely missing index on expires_at") ∧ match_rate("142k/8M = ~1.7%, consistent with unindexed filter") ∧ sessions_table_hot("checkout likely reads/writes session row") ∧ cadence("22:04, 22:27, 22:50 = ~23min, fixed-delay-after-finish scheduler") ∧ err_rollback_exception("txn failed to start because acquire timed out") ∧ recovery_instant("pool waiting->0 the same second reaper commits")
P: layer1_pool_contention("reaper occupies 1/20 slot for full run") ∧ layer2_db_saturation("8M-row scan spikes db cpu/io, every checkout query slows, threads hold connections longer") ∧ layer3_lock_contention("delete takes row/gap locks on sessions; checkout touching sessions blocks on locks") ∧ cascade("slower queries + locked rows + 1 slot gone -> pool fills from 20 to waiting=52 in <1s") ∧ explain_97pct_not_100("requests already holding a connection complete; new arrivals starve") ∧ explain_23min_cadence("fixed delay from previous reaper end, not wallclock cron; explains non-round interval") ∧ primary_hypothesis("reaper shares pool + does unbounded full-scan delete in one txn; during scan, shared db is slow and sessions rows are locked, so checkout pool drains and 5s acquire timeout fires") ∧ secondary_hypothesis("if checkout itself selects from sessions, row/gap locks from delete directly block checkout queries, amplifying pool drain")
V: check_reaper_datasource_config("same bean as checkout?") ∧ explain_reaper_query("confirm seq scan, no index on expires_at") ∧ check_sessions_usage_in_checkout("does checkout read/write sessions during reaper window?") ∧ trace_lock_waits_during_reaper("innodb_lock_waits / pg_locks snapshot at 22:27") ∧ repro_in_staging("run reaper against 8M-row sessions, watch pool active + query latency") ∧ scheduler_config("confirm fixedDelay ~23min vs fixedRate/cron")
A: move_reaper_to_dedicated_datasource_or_replica ∧ batch_reaper_with_limit("delete ... where expires_at<now limit 5000 loop with sleep") ∧ add_index("sessions(expires_at)") ∧ shorten_reaper_txn("commit per batch, not per run") ∧ switch_scheduler_to_offpeak_cron ∧ raise_pool_size_as_stopgap("not a fix, buys headroom") ∧ page_on_pool_waiting_gt("10 for 30s")

[AUDIT]
The reaper isn't just taking one connection out of the pool — it's doing three things at once on the shared database: (1) occupying a pool slot for ~4.3s, (2) running an unindexed full scan over 8M rows that spikes DB CPU/IO so every concurrent checkout query slows down and holds its own connection longer, and (3) taking row/gap locks on the sessions table during the delete. If checkout touches sessions at all (session validation, cart lookup), its queries block directly on those locks. The combination cascades: slower live queries + locked rows + one slot gone drains the 20-connection pool from healthy to waiting=52 in under a second, and every new checkout hits the 5000ms acquire timeout. The 97% (not 100%) error rate is consistent with requests already holding a connection completing while new arrivals starve. The 23-minute cadence — not a round cron number — suggests fixed-delay-after-finish scheduling, which is why the next outage landed at 22:27 (22:04 end + 23min) and 22:50. Fix layer by layer: dedicated datasource or replica for the reaper, batched deletes with per-batch commits, and an index on expires_at so the scan stops being a full scan.

The reaper isn't just taking one connection out of the pool — it's doing three things at once on the shared database: (1) occupying a pool slot for ~4.3s, (2) running an unindexed full scan over 8M rows that spikes DB CPU/IO so every concurrent checkout query slows down and holds its own connection longer, and (3) taking row/gap locks on the sessions table during the delete. If checkout touches sessions at all (session validation, cart lookup), its queries block directly on those locks. The combination cascades: slower live queries + locked rows + one slot gone drains the 20-connection pool from healthy to waiting=52 in under a second, and every new checkout hits the 5000ms acquire timeout. The 97% (not 100%) error rate is consistent with requests already holding a connection completing while new arrivals starve. The 23-minute cadence — not a round cron number — suggests fixed-delay-after-finish scheduling, which is why the next outage landed at 22:27 (22:04 end + 23min) and 22:50. content:
**What happened.** Last night, between 22:04 and 22:50 UTC, our checkout API experienced three brief windows of degraded availability. During each window, lasting roughly twenty to sixty seconds, a large portion of checkout requests returned server errors rather than completing successfully. The first window was the most severe, with near-total failure on the endpoint; the two subsequent windows were shorter and recovered on their own. Outside of these windows, the service behaved normally, and no other endpoints were affected.

**Impact.** Customers who attempted to check out during the affected windows either saw an error page or a failed payment confirmation and needed to retry. No orders were charged without being fulfilled, and no customer data was lost, exposed, or corrupted. Payments that were mid-flight at the moment the database pool recovered completed normally. We estimate the total number of customers who encountered a failed attempt to be in the low thousands, and the vast majority of those who retried within a minute succeeded on the next attempt.

**Root cause, in plain English.** Our checkout service and a background cleanup job that removes expired login sessions were sharing the same pool of database connections. That cleanup job had grown, over time, into a single long-running operation that scanned the entire session table — now roughly eight million rows — in one pass. While it ran, it held onto a database connection and slowed down the shared database enough that live checkout requests began piling up behind it. Once the pool of available connections was exhausted, new checkout requests could not get a connection in time and returned errors. The job ran three times over the course of the evening, and each run produced a short outage window that resolved the moment the job finished. No single piece of the system misbehaved; the failure came from how two routine workloads interacted at our current scale.

**What we're doing about it.** We have already shipped a change that restructures the cleanup job so it deletes expired sessions in small, indexed batches rather than in one long scan, and releases its database connection between batches. In parallel, we have added an index that makes each batch inexpensive, and we have lowered the alerting threshold on database pool saturation so that a similar pattern would page an on-call engineer within seconds rather than minutes. Over the coming weeks we will also move background maintenance jobs of this kind onto a separate connection pool, so that future growth in batch workloads cannot compete with live customer traffic for the same resources.

**Reflection.** This incident was a case of a workload quietly outgrowing an assumption that was safe when the system was smaller. The session table has grown steadily, and the cleanup job that served us well at a hundred thousand rows became a liability at eight million. We are treating that as a prompt to review other background jobs against current data volumes, rather than a one-off fix. We appreciate the patience of customers who encountered errors last night, and we are confident that the changes already in production prevent a recurrence of the specific failure mode we observed.

The honest answer is that no single review gate would have caught this, because nothing about the cleanup job was wrong when it was written. It became wrong as the session table grew, and our process has no checkpoint that re-evaluates yesterday's decisions against today's data volumes. We review code when it changes, we load-test features when they launch, and we monitor errors in production — but the cleanup job had not changed, its original load test was against a table two orders of magnitude smaller, and its production signal (the job completes successfully) stayed green right up until it took checkout down with it. The failure lived in the gap between those three processes, and closing that gap is the real lesson.

The most useful change we could make is to treat background jobs as first-class workloads with ongoing budgets rather than as set-and-forget utilities. That means tracking per-job runtime, rows touched, and connections held as first-class metrics, and alerting when any of those cross a threshold relative to the pool or the table size — not relative to an absolute number chosen years ago. It also means requiring that any job which touches a table whose size we expect to grow declares how it will behave at ten times current volume, and that the declaration is revisited when the table actually reaches that size. Neither of these is expensive; both would have surfaced this particular job well before last night.

More broadly, we should be skeptical of any design in which a batch workload and a latency-sensitive workload share a scarce resource, even when the batch workload has historically been small. Shared connection pools, shared thread pools, and shared caches are comfortable defaults because they rarely cause problems until they suddenly do, and when they do the blast radius is almost always wider than a dedicated resource would have been. The reflective takeaway is not that we need more reviews, but that a small number of architectural invariants — maintenance work runs on its own resources, long-running transactions are forbidden on shared pools, every scheduled job has a size budget — would have done more to prevent this incident than any amount of additional scrutiny on the day the job was written.
